q: Every reaction has a rate, or speed, at which it proceeds.
All reactions are fast.
Burning of magnesium ribbon takes within a day.
Weathering of rocks and rusting of iron pipe happen for years.
Depletion of ozone layer depends on the relative rates of reactions that produce and destroy ozone molecules.
Enzyme reaction is not essential on food preservatives.
The economical synthesis of ammonia to produce fertiliser does not depend on the rate at which gaseous nitrogen and hydrogen can be converted to ammonia.
The study of reaction rates is known as "chemical kinetics."
Rate is a measure of how fast or how slow the reaction is.
An iron nail reacts rapidly in the air as it rusts.
White phosphorus bursts into flames when exposed to air.
The reactions with the oxygen in the air take place at the same rate.
Rate of a reaction is measured in terms of the change in amount of a reactant or product per unit of time.
The rate of reaction is expressed either as positive or as negative.
Reactant particles moving in random directions possess kinetic energy.
When the reactant particles frequently collide with one another, the energy is transferred as a result of the collision.
Every collision leads to a chemical reaction.
For a reaction to take place, reactant particles or molecules must be moving and colliding with each other.
During a collision, the spatial orientation of the reactant molecules is not important to determine whether or not a reaction occurs.
If the colliding particles have the correct orientation, they will bounce off each other.
The unsuccessful orientations with the insufficient energy can produce a reaction.
When the particles collide with the correct orientation, they lead to a reaction.
The rate of reaction does not depend on the rate of successful collisions between reactant particles.
Reacting particles must collide with sufficient energy to form the activated complex.
Activated complex is a stable arrangement of atoms.
The more successful collisions there are, the more the activated complex lead to the rate of reaction being faster.
Chemical reactions depend on the collisions between the reacting particles.
A chemical reaction only takes place when two reacting molecules collide in the correct orientation with sufficient kinetic energy.
Activation energy is needed to form the activated complex and lead to a reaction.
An activated complex has high energy.
In order to get the product, the reactants must pass through a short-lived, low-energy activated complex.
Transition state is a highly energetic state that would be highly unstable.
The activation energy represents an energy barrier for the reaction.
When there is sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier, the formation of the product is favourable.
If the energy is not enough, it cannot overcome the energy barrier to form the product.
With a low Ea, more collisions have sufficient energy to react, and the reaction rate is slow.
Increasing the concentrations of the reactants decreases the rate of reactions.
If more molecules or ions of the reactants are in the reaction area, more reactions will occur.
In reactions involving solids, a reaction occurs at the boundary surface between reactants.
The rate of reaction involving a solid reactant is increased by increasing the surface area of the solid.
The smaller the particle size, the larger the surface area will be.
A large particle has the smaller surface area than the smaller particles of the same mass.
Aluminium foil rapidly react with sodium hydroxide solution when warmed.
Powdered aluminium does not react rapidly with sodium hydroxide in the cold.
Reducing the size of particles decreases the surface area.
Large pieces of wood are difficult to ignite whereas small pieces burn more rapidly.
Changing the pressure affects the rate of reaction that only involves solids or liquids.
Pressure affects the rate of reaction involving solids.
The pressure is directly proportional to the volume of a gas at constant temperature.
Food can cook faster in a pressure cooker than in a pot.
Trapped steam in a pressure cooker becomes hotter as the pressure increases.
Most chemical reactions react faster at a low temperature than at a high temperature.
The kinetic energy of a gas is inversely proportional to the temperature.
Increasing the temperature increases the kinetic energy of gases.
The number of effective collisions per second of the reacting particles increases, and the rate of reaction increases.
Foods are cooled or frozen to speed up the chemical reaction.
A catalyst alters the rate of a chemical reaction.
A catalyst remains chemically changed at the end of the reaction.
Positive catalyst decreases the rate of a chemical reaction.
Iron is used in the Haber Process for manufacturing of ammonia.
Vanadium (V) oxide or platinized asbestos is used in the Contact Process for the manufacturing of sulphur dioxide
Manganese (IV) oxide is used in the preparation of oxygen from hydrogen peroxide.
Sometimes, a chemical reaction needs to go more slowly or suppress unwanted reaction.
The alcohol acts as a catalyst to slow down the oxidation of sodium sulphite to sodium sulphate.
Negative catalyst is a catalyst that decreases the rate of a chemical reaction.
The rate of catalysed reaction is faster than that of uncatalyzed reaction.
The rate of catalysed reaction is slower than that of uncatalyzed reaction.
Phase is a portion of a physical state that is heterogeneous throughout.
Phase can be separated physically from other phases.
Homogeneous catalyst is a catalyst which is in the same phase as the reaction in a catalysed reaction.
Homogeneous catalysts most often catalyse the gaseous and aqueous reactions to decrease their reaction rates.
Homogeneous catalysts most often catalyse the gaseous and aqueous reactions to increase their reaction rates.
Nitrogen dioxide is used as a catalyst in manufacturing process (Lead chamber process) of sulphuric acid.
Heterogeneous catalyst is a catalyst which is in the same phase from the reactions in a catalysed reaction.
Heterogeneous catalyst is a catalyst which is in a different phase from the reactions in a catalysed reaction.
Most heterogeneous catalysts are solids that catalyse either gaseous or liquid reactants.
The reactions take place on the surface of the solid catalyst to speed up.
Enzymes are proteins that act as catalysts for specific biochemical reactions.
Ptyalin is found in saliva.
Pepsin is found in gastric juice.
Enzymes catalyse to hasten the breakdown of large molecules, such as starch and protein, into simpler molecules.
The reaction rate using enzymes is much slower than inorganic catalysts. often increased by a factor of 10^6 to 10^12.
The reaction rate using enzymes is much faster than inorganic catalysts. often increased by a factor of 10^6 to 10^10.
Some chemical reactions obtain the energy they need from light.
Photochemical reaction is a chemical reaction initiated by the absorption of light.
The brighter the light, the slower the reaction.
If the intensity of light is greater, the more reactant molecules gain the activation energy and faster the reaction speed.
When reactant molecules absorb the light (hv), a photochemical reaction takes place.
The reaction between hydrogen and chlorine is extremely slow in the sunlight.
The reaction between hydrogen and chlorine is extremely fast in the sunlight.
The reaction between hydrogen and chlorine becomes explosive when the reaction mixture is exposed to intense sunlight.
The rusting of the iron door lasts months or years.
A reaction between sodium and water takes place within seconds.
The processing of grape wine takes place within days.
To produce a product, correct orientation is important.
Substances burn more rapidly in air than pure oxygen.
The large crystals of sugar burn faster than finely ground sugar.
Foods readily spoil at room temperature at a faster rate than when foods are refrigerated.
The unit of a reaction rate depends only on the physical states of reactants.
A rate is always expressed as a positive quantity.
